folkvangr.residents: Half of those who die in battle

helheim.description: Cold, misty, neither reward nor punishment
helheim.residents: Those who die of sickness, old age, not in battle

valhalla.hall: 540 doors, shields for roof, spears for rafters
valhalla.activities: Daily combat, feasting, preparation for Ragnarok
valhalla.residents: Warriors who died in battle (chosen by Valkyries)

eschatology.ragnarok: Twilight of gods - end and rebirth of world
eschatology.survival: Few gods and humans survive to new world

keyMyths.title: Valhalla: The Hall of the Slain
keyMyths.description: Valhalla is Odin's hall in Asgard, reserved for warriors who die bravely in battle. Chosen by the Valkyries, these einherjar feast and train, preparing for Ragnarok, the final battle.

keyMyths.title: Fólkvangr: Freyja's Field
keyMyths.description: Fólkvangr is Freyja's realm, where she receives half of those who die in battle, the other half going to Odin's Valhalla. This realm suggests a more egalitarian view of the afterlife, not solely based on martial prowess.

keyMyths.title: Helheim: The Realm of Hel
keyMyths.description: Helheim is ruled by Hel, a daughter of Loki, and is the destination for those who die of old age or disease, or are otherwise not chosen for Valhalla or Fólkvangr. It's often depicted as a gloomy and cold place, though not necessarily a place of torment.

keyMyths.title: Niflheim: The Primordial Realm of Ice
keyMyths.description: While not strictly an afterlife, Niflheim, the realm of primordial ice and darkness, is associated with death and the origin of the world. It's a cold and desolate place, representing the void before creation.

keyMyths.title: Ragnarok: The Fate of the Gods and the World's Renewal
keyMyths.description: Ragnarok, the twilight of the gods, involves a cataclysmic battle that destroys the world, including many of the afterlife realms. However, from the ashes, a new and fertile world emerges, suggesting a cyclical view of death and rebirth.

sources.name: The Poetic Edda
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, including mythological and heroic tales, providing primary source material for Norse mythology.
sources.title: The Poetic Edda

sources.name: The Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson)
sources.description: Written by Snorri Sturluson in the 13th century, this work retells and interprets Norse myths, offering a comprehensive overview of the mythology.
sources.title: The Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson)

sources.name: Hilda Ellis Davidson, Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: A comprehensive academic study of Norse mythology, exploring the different gods, myths, and religious practices of the Norse people.
sources.title: Hilda Ellis Davidson, Gods and Myths of Northern Europe

sources.name: Neil Price, The Viking Way: Religion and War in Late Iron Age Scandinavia
sources.description: An analysis of Viking-age religion, including the role of warfare and beliefs about the afterlife.
sources.title: Neil Price, The Viking Way: Religion and War in Late Iron Age Scandinavia

sources.name: John Lindow, Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs
sources.description: A detailed guide to Norse mythology, covering a wide range of topics, including the gods, heroes, rituals, and beliefs of the Norse people.
sources.title: John Lindow, Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs

relatedConcepts.name: From Ginnungagap to the First Humans
